blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (2)
مَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَمَّآ أُنذِرُوا۟ مُعْرِضُونَ (٣)
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term. Yet the faithless are disregardful of what they are warned. (3)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ۖ ٱئْتُونِى بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ أَثَـٰرَةٍ مِّنْ عِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤)
Say, ‘Tell me about those you invoke besides Allah. Show me what [part] of the earth have they created. Do they have any share in the heavens? Bring me a scripture [revealed] before this, or some vestige of [divine] knowledge, if you are truthful.’ (4)
وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّن يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَن لَّا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَهُمْ عَن دُعَآئِهِمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٥)
Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such [entities] as would not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and who are oblivious of their invocation? (5)
وَإِذَا حُشِرَ ٱلنَّاسُ كَانُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَكَانُوا۟ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٦)
When mankind are mustered [on Judgement’s Day] they will be their enemies, and they will disavow their worship. (6)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, the faithless say of the truth when it comes to them: ‘This is plain magic.’ (7)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَلَا تَمْلِكُونَ لِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۖ كَفَىٰ بِهِۦ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٨)
Or they say, ‘He has fabricated it.’ Say, ‘Should I have fabricated it, you would not avail me anything against Allah. He best knows what you gossip concerning it. He suffices as a witness between me and you, and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’ (8)
قُلْ مَا كُنتُ بِدْعًا مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَمَآ أَدْرِى مَا يُفْعَلُ بِى وَلَا بِكُمْ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٩)
Say, ‘I am not a novelty among the apostles, nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you. I just follow whatever is revealed to me, and I am just a manifest warner.’ (9)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ مِثْلِهِۦ فَـَٔامَنَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed [in it], while you are disdainful [of it]?’ Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (10)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْ كَانَ خَيْرًا مَّا سَبَقُونَآ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ (١١)
The faithless say about the faithful, ‘Had it been [anything] good, they would not have taken the lead over us toward [accepting] it.’ And since they could not find the way to it, they will say, ‘It is an ancient lie.’ (11)
وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّسَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢)
Yet before it the Book of Moses was a guide and mercy, and this is a Book in the Arabic language, which confirms it, [sent] to warn the wrongdoers, and is a [bearer of] good news for the virtuous. (12)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١٣)
Those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah,’ and then remain steadfast, they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (13)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤)
They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, remaining in it [forever]—a reward for what they used to do. (14)

(Commentary)

The purpose of this Surah is to warn the polytheists (المشركين) who reject the call to faith in Allah and His Messenger regarding the Resurrection (المعاد), including the painful punishment awaiting those who deny it and turn away from it. For this reason, the discourse opens by establishing the Resurrection: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom (بالحق)" [Qarai: 46:3]. Then, the discourse returns to this theme repeatedly, as in His saying: وَإِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ — "When the people are mustered" [Qarai: 46:6]; and His saying: وَالَّذِي قالَ لِوالِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَكُما أَتَعِدانِنِي أَنْ أُخْرَجَ — "As for him who says to his parents, ‘Fie on you! Do you promise me that I shall be raised [from the dead]’" [Qarai: 46:17]; and His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be told,] ‘You exhausted your good things’" [Qarai: 46:20]; and His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِ — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be asked,] ‘Is this not a fact?’" [Qarai: 46:34]; and His saying at the conclusion of the Surah: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ بَلاغٌ — "On the day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] but for an hour of a day. This is a proclamation" [Qarai: 46:35].

The Surah also contains arguments for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and prophethood (النبوة). It alludes to the destruction of the people of Hud and the destruction of the towns around Mecca as a warning to them. It also reports the presence of a group of jinn before the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, their listening to the Quran, their belief in it, and their return to their people as warners.

The Surah is entirely Meccan except for two verses regarding which there is disagreement; we shall point them out in the forthcoming "Discussion of Narrations," if Allah wills. These are His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’?" [Qarai: 46:8], and His saying: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ — "Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah’" [Qarai: 46:10].

His saying, the Exalted: حم تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ — "Ha, Meem. The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" [Qarai: 46:1-2]. Its interpretation has been provided previously.

His saying, the Exalted: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a stated term" [Qarai: 46:3]. What is meant by "the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" is the totality of the witnessed world (العالم المشهود), both its celestial and terrestrial realms. The preposition "with" (بـ) in "with truth" (بالحق) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة). The "stated term" (الأجل المسمى) refers to the point at which the duration of a thing's existence ends. In this verse, it refers to the stated term for the existence of the entire world, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), wherein the heaven shall be rolled up (تطوى) like the rolling up of scrolls for writings, and the earth shall be replaced (تبدل) with another earth and the heavens, and they shall emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount.

The meaning is: We did not create the witnessed world with all its celestial and terrestrial parts except as accompanied by truth (الحق) — having a fixed purpose — and accompanied by a specific term (أجل معين) that its existence does not exceed. Since it has a specific term at which it will perish, and yet possesses a fixed purpose, then after this world there must be another world, which is the world of permanence (عالم البقاء); this is the promised Resurrection. The discussion regarding the meaning of creation being "with truth" has been repeated in what preceded.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَمَّا أُنْذِرُوا مُعْرِضُونَ — "But the faithless are oblivious of what they are warned" [Qarai: 46:3]. Those who disbelieve (الذين كفروا) refers to the polytheists, as evidenced by the following verse, but the apparent context suggests that their disbelief refers to their denial of the Resurrection. The word "what" (ما) in "of what" (عما) is either a gerundive (مصدرية) or a relative pronoun (موصولة); the latter is more consistent with the context. The meaning is: The polytheists who disbelieve in the Resurrection are turning away (معرضون) and diverting themselves from that which they have been warned of — namely, the Day of Resurrection and the painful punishment it holds for those who associate partners with Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "Say, ‘Tell me about those you invoke besides Allah’" [Qarai: 46:4] until the end of the verse. "Tell me" (أرأيتم) means "inform me." What is meant by "those you invoke besides Allah" are the idols they used to call upon and worship. The use of pronouns referring to rational beings (أولي العقل) for them later in the verse is because the polytheists attributed the actions of rational beings to them. Nevertheless, the argument of the verse and what follows it applies to every deity worshipped besides Allah.

His saying: أَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ — "Show me what they have created of the earth" [Qarai: 46:4]. "Show me" (أروني) means "inform me." "What" (ما) is an interrogative noun, and "that" (ذا) after it is pleonastic; the combination is the object of "they created" (خلقوا), and "of the earth" (من الأرض) is related to it.

His saying: أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّماواتِ — "Or do they have a share in [the creation of] the heavens?" [Qarai: 46:4]. That is, a partnership (شركة) in the creation of the heavens, for the creation of something in the heavens or the earth is what is being questioned.

To clarify: although they did not attribute anything to the idols except the management (تدبير) of the cosmos and reserved creation (الخلق) for Him, the Glorified — as He, the Exalted, said: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ — "If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah’" [Qarai: 39:38], and He said: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ — "If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah’" [Qarai: 43:87] — yet, since creation is inseparable from management, it follows that whoever has a share in management must have a share in creation. Therefore, Allah commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask them about the portion their lords, whom they invoke besides Allah, have in the creation of the earth or the heavens; for management in the cosmos is meaningless without creation.

His saying: ائْتُونِي بِكِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا أَوْ أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Bring me a scripture [revealed] before this, or some vestige of knowledge, if you are truthful" [Qarai: 46:4]. The word "this" (هذا) refers to the Quran. What is meant by "a scripture before this" is a heavenly book like the Torah, sent down from Allah, that mentions the partnership of their deities in the creation of the heavens or the earth.

The word vestige (الأثارة), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is a verbal noun meaning transmission (النقل) and narration (الرواية). He said: "I transmitted the knowledge (أثرت العلم)" means "I narrated it (رويته)." Its root is "I followed its track (تتبعت أثره)." Accordingly, "vestige" (الأثارة) in the verse is a verbal noun used in the sense of the object (اسم مفعول), meaning something transmitted from knowledge that proves their deities have a partnership in anything in the heavens or the earth. Most commentators have interpreted it to mean "remnant" (بقية), which is close to the aforementioned meaning.

The meaning is: Bring me, to demonstrate their partnership with Allah in the creation of anything on earth or in the heavens, a heavenly book before the Quran that mentions this, or something transmitted from knowledge, or a remnant of knowledge you have inherited that proves this, if you are truthful in your claim that they are partners with Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مَنْ لا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ — "Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such [entities] as will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection" [Qarai: 46:5]. The interrogation is rhetorical (إنكاري). The limitation of their non-responsiveness to the Day of Resurrection is because the Day of Resurrection is the stated term for this world, and invocation is confined to this world; there is no world after the standing of the Hour.

His saying: وَهُمْ عَنْ دُعائِهِمْ غافِلُونَ — "and who are oblivious of their invocation?" [Qarai: 46:5]. This is another attribute of their deities added to the attribute of non-responsiveness; it is not a causal explanation for the lack of response. The lack of response is caused by the fact that they possess nothing for their worshippers, as He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً — "Say, ‘Do you worship besides Allah what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm?’" [Qarai: 5:76].

Rather, it is an attribute added to the mentioned one to serve as a preamble and preparation for what He will mention in the following verse regarding their enmity toward them and their denial of their worship on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, in this world, they are oblivious (غافلون) of their invocation, but they will become aware of it on the Day of Resurrection, whereupon they will show enmity toward them and disbelieve in their worship.

The verse contains an indication of the pervasiveness of life and consciousness (الشعور) in all things, even inanimate objects (الجمادات). For idols are inanimate, yet "obliviousness" (الغفلة) has been attributed to them, and obliviousness is a state of conscious beings; it is only applied to that which is naturally capable of consciousness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْداءً وَكانُوا بِعِبادَتِهِمْ كافِرِينَ — "When the people are mustered, they will be their enemies, and they will disavow their worship" [Qarai: 46:6]. Mustering (الحشر) is the removal of a thing from its place with agitation. What is meant is the resurrection of people from their graves and their being driven to the gathering place on the Day of Resurrection. On that day, their deities will be their enemies and will disbelieve in the polytheism of their worshippers by disassociating from them, as He, the Exalted, said: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ — "and on the Day of Resurrection they will disown your polytheism" [Qarai: 35:14]. And He said, narrating their words: تَبَرَّأْنا إِلَيْكَ ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ — "We disavow them before You: it was not us that they used to worship" [Qarai: 28:63]. And He said: فَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً بَيْننا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ — "Allah suffices as a witness between us and you: we were indeed oblivious of your worship" [Qarai: 10:29].

The context of these two verses hints that these inanimate objects, which do not appear to us in this life to possess life because their effects are not manifest, will be shown in the next life to possess life and their effects will appear. Some discussion on this meaning has preceded under His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, [He] who gives speech to all things’" [Qarai: 41:21].

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ — "When Our manifest signs are recited to them, the faithless say of the truth when it comes to them: ‘This is plain magic’" [Qarai: 46:7]. This verse and the one following it are intended for rebuke. "Manifest signs" (الآيات البينات) refers to the verses of the Quran being recited to them. Then, He substituted them with "the truth" (الحق) that came to them when He said: "of the truth when it comes to them" — whereas the apparent requirement was to say "of them" (لها). This is to indicate that they are a truth that has come to them, and there is no justification for labeling them as "plain magic" while they know they are manifest truth. Thus, they are being arbitrary and obstinate against the explicit truth.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَلا تَمْلِكُونَ لِي مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Say, ‘If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me anything against Allah’" [Qarai: 46:8]. The word "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة), meaning: "Nay, they say he has fabricated the Quran against Allah in his claim that it is His speech."

His saying: قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَلا تَمْلِكُونَ لِي مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً — "Say, ‘If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me anything against Allah’" [Qarai: 46:8]. That is: If I have fabricated the Quran for your sake, He would seize me with punishment or hasten my punishment for the fabrication, and you would not be able to repel His punishment from me. The gist is: I am certain of Allah's affair, and I know that He seizes the fabricator or hastens his punishment, and that you cannot repel what He intends. So how could I fabricate against Him and expose myself to His certain punishment for your sake? This means: I am not a fabricator against Him.

It becomes clear from this that the apodosis (جزاء الشرط) in His saying: "If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me..." is omitted, and what serves as the removal of an obstacle has been put in its place. The estimation is: "If I have fabricated it, He would seize me with punishment, and there is no obstacle on your part to prevent it." It is not a case of putting the effect in place of the cause, as has been suggested.

His saying: هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ — "He knows best what you are engaged in" [Qarai: 46:8]. Engaging (الإفاضة) in speech means plunging into it. "What" (بما) is either a relative pronoun referring to the Quran, or a gerundive. The meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, knows best that which you are plunging into of denial by labeling the Quran as magic and fabrication against Allah. Or the meaning is: He knows best your plunging into the Quran.

His saying: كَفى بِهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ — "He suffices as a witness between me and you" [Qarai: 46:8]. This is a second argument for the negation of fabrication. The first of the two arguments was His saying: "If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me anything against Allah." The meaning of the sentence is: Allah's testimony in His speech that it is His speech and not a fabrication from me suffices to negate my being a fabricator against Him. Allah, the Glorified, has confirmed this claim in His saying: لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ — "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you — He sent it down with His knowledge" [Qarai: 4:166], and similar verses. As for it being His speech, the verses of challenge (آيات التحدي) suffice for its establishment.

His saying: وَهُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ — "and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" [Qarai: 46:8]. The conclusion of the verse with these two noble names is an argument to negate what their false arbitrariness implies regarding the denial of the Message (الرسالة) — for the idolaters deny it absolutely. It is as if it were said: Your saying "He fabricated it" involves two claims: the claim that this Quran is not the speech of Allah, and the claim that the Message is invalid. As for the first claim, it is refuted first by: "If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me...", and second by: "Allah suffices me as a witness that it is His speech, not mine."

As for the second claim, it is refuted by the fact that Allah, the Glorified, is All-forgiving and All-merciful. It is necessary in His wisdom that He treats His creation with forgiveness and mercy. These do not encompass any except the repentant who return to Him and are righteous, and that is by Him guiding them to a path that brings them closer to Him through its traversal. Thus, His forgiveness and mercy encompass them by the removal of sins and settlement in the abode of eternal happiness. This is necessary in His wisdom because they possess the potential for this perfection, and He is the All-generous, the All-bountiful. He, the Exalted, said: وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً — "and the bounty of your Lord is not off-limits" [Qarai: 17:20], and He said: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ — "With Allah rests the [guidance to the] straight path" [Qarai: 16:9]. The way to this guidance is the call through the Message. Therefore, it is necessary in wisdom that He sends a Messenger to the people to call them to His path that leads to His forgiveness and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما كُنْتُ بِدْعاً مِنَ الرُّسُلِ وَما أَدْرِي ما يُفْعَلُ بِي وَلا بِكُمْ — "Say, ‘I am not a novelty among the apostles, nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you’" [Qarai: 46:9]. Novelty (البدع) refers to that which was not preceded by a likeness in terms of its attributes, or its words and deeds. Therefore, some have interpreted the meaning as: "I am not the first messenger sent to you, with no messenger before me." Others said the meaning is: "I am not an innovator in my words and deeds, such that no messenger preceded me in them."

The first meaning does not suit the context nor His preceding saying: "and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" in the sense we have explained. Thus, the second meaning is more appropriate. Accordingly, the meaning is: I do not differ from the previous messengers in form or conduct, nor in word or deed; rather, I am a human being like them, possessing the same human traits they possessed, and their way of life is my way.

With this sentence, an answer is provided to such claims as Allah narrated from them: ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْفِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً أَوْ يُلْقى إِلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْها — "They say, ‘What sort of apostle is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner along with him? Or [why is not] a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’" [Qarai: 25:7-8].

His saying: وَما أَدْرِي ما يُفْعَلُ بِي وَلا بِكُمْ — "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you" [Qarai: 46:9]. This is a negation of the knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — from himself. It is thus similar to His saying: وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ وَما مَسَّنِيَ السُّوءُ — "Had I known the Unseen, I would have surely amassed a lot of good, and no ill would have touched me" [Qarai: 7:188]. The difference between the two verses is that His saying: "Had I known the Unseen..." is a negation of the knowledge of the Unseen in an absolute sense, using the touch of evil and the lack of amassing good as evidence. His saying: "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you" is a negation of the knowledge of a specific Unseen, namely the events that will befall him and them, which they all face. This is because they presumed that one who is clothed in prophethood, if there were a prophet, must inherently know the Unseen and possess absolute Unseen power, as appears from their suggestions narrated in the Quran. Thus, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to confess — explicitly — that he does not know what will be done with him or with them, thereby negating from himself the knowledge of the Unseen. Furthermore, it implies that the events that occur to him and them are outside his will and choice, and he has no hand in any of them; rather, another does them to him and them, and that is Allah, the Glorified.

Thus, His saying: "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you," just as it negates from him the knowledge of the Unseen, it also negates from him the power over anything that befalls him or them from that which is behind the veils of the Unseen.

The verse's negation of the knowledge of the Unseen from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله does not contradict his knowledge of the Unseen through the medium of revelation (الوحي), as He, the Exalted, explicitly states in various places of His speech, such as His saying: ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ — "These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you" [Qarai: 3:44, 12:102]; and His saying: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيها إِلَيْكَ — "These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you" [Qarai: 11:49]; and His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" [Qarai: 72:26-27]. From this category is the saying of the Messiah عليه‌السلام: وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَما تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ — "and I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up in your houses" [Qarai: 3:49]; and the saying of Joseph عليه‌السلام to his two companions in prison: لا يَأْتِيكُما طَعامٌ تُرْزَقانِهِ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُما بِتَأْوِيلِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُما — "No food that you are provided with shall come to you but that I will inform you of its interpretation before it comes to you" [Qarai: 12:37].

The reason there is no contradiction is that the verses negating the knowledge of the Unseen from him and from the other prophets عليهم‌السلام only negate it from their human nature (طبيعتهم البشرية). That is, they negate that they possess a human nature, or a nature higher than human nature, whose inherent property is the knowledge of the Unseen such that they could use it to bring every benefit and repel every harm, just as we use what we obtain through ordinary means. This does not contradict the Unseen being disclosed to them through divine teaching via revelation, just as their performing miracles (المعجزات) was not from a psychological power they possessed for themselves, but by the permission and command of Allah. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً — "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human apostle?’" [Qarai: 17:93], in response to the signs they suggested to him. And He said: قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَإِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — "Say, ‘The signs are only with Allah, and I am only a manifest warner’" [Qarai: 29:50]. And He said: وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ فَإِذا جاءَ أَمْرُ اللهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ — "No apostle may bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. But when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice" [Qarai: 40:78].

This is evidenced by His saying immediately following it: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ — "I only follow what is revealed to me" [Qarai: 46:9]. Its connection to what preceded indicates that it is in the position of an antithesis (إضراب). The meaning is: I do not know anything of these events through the Unseen from myself; I only follow what is revealed to me regarding that.

His saying: وَما أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — "and I am only a manifest warner" [Qarai: 46:9]. This is a confirmation of all that preceded in the verse from His saying: "I am not a novelty..." etc., and "nor do I know..." etc., and His saying: "I only follow..." etc.

(A Philosophical Discussion and Refutation of a Doubt)

Narrations have been transmitted through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt that Allah, the Glorified, taught the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams عليهم‌السلام the knowledge of everything. In some of these, it is explained that the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله knowledge was through revelation and that the Imams' عليهم‌السلام knowledge traces back to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

An objection has been raised: What is recorded of their biography is that they lived throughout their lives like other people, pursuing their goals by striving for them according to what external causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) indicate and ordinary paths (السبل العادية) guide to. Sometimes they achieved their goals, and sometimes the path failed them and they did not. Had they known the Unseen, they would never have failed in their striving; for a rational person does not abandon a path he knows with certainty he will succeed in, nor does he traverse a path he knows with certainty he will fail in.

Furthermore, they were afflicted with calamities that it is not permissible for a person to throw himself into if he knew the reality of the matter. For example, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was afflicted on the day of Uhud, Ali عليه‌السلام was struck in the Mosque of Kufa when Ibn Muljam al-Muradi (may Allah curse him) assassinated him, al-Husayn عليه‌السلام was killed in Karbala, and the other Imams were afflicted by poison. If they knew what would happen to them, that would be throwing oneself into destruction (التهلكة), which is forbidden. The objection, as you see, is taken from the two verses: "Had I known the Unseen, I would have surely amassed a lot of good..." and "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you."

The refutation is that this is a fallacy (مغالطة) resulting from confusing ordinary knowledge (العلوم العادية) with non-ordinary knowledge. Non-ordinary knowledge of the realities of things has no effect on changing the course of external events.

To clarify: our voluntary acts (أفعالنا الاختيارية), just as they relate to our will, also relate to other material, temporal, and spatial causes and conditions. When those causes and conditions converge and are completed by the will, the complete cause (العلة التامة) is realized, and the occurrence of the act at that point becomes necessary and certain (واجباً ضرورياً); for it is impossible for the effect to fail to follow its complete cause.

Thus, the relation of the act — which is an effect — to its complete cause is one of necessity and certainty (الوجوب والضرورة), just like the relation of all events to their complete causes. Its relation to our will — which is a part of its cause — is one of possibility and contingency (الجواز والإمكان).

It becomes clear that all external events, including our voluntary acts, are necessary to occur in the external world, happening therein with the quality of certainty. This does not contradict our voluntary acts being contingent (ممكنة) in relation to us, despite their necessity as explained.

Since every event, including our voluntary acts in their voluntary quality, is an effect that has a complete cause with which its failure to occur is impossible, then events are an ordered chain encompassed by necessity. No link in this chain can exceed its place, nor can it be exchanged for another. Everything was necessary from the first day, whether what occurred in the past or what has not yet occurred. Therefore, if we assume the attainment of knowledge of the realities of events as they are in the core of reality, this would not affect removing any event from them — even if voluntary — from the realm of necessity to the limit of contingency.

If you say: Rather, this certain knowledge falls within the course of the causes of voluntary acts, like the knowledge obtained from ordinary methods. Thus, it would be utilized when it contradicts the knowledge obtained from ordinary methods, becoming a cause for action or omission, whereby the ordinary knowledge is nullified.

I say: Not at all. For the assumption is the realization of the complete cause for the ordinary knowledge along with the other causes of the voluntary act. Its likeness is that of the people of denial and obstinacy among the disbelievers; they are certain that their end with denial is the Fire, yet they persist in their denial due to the judgment of their whims regarding the necessity of denial. This, on their part, is the ordinary knowledge of the necessity of the act. He, the Exalted, said regarding the story of the people of Pharaoh: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "They impugned them — though they were convinced in their hearts" [Qarai: 27:14].

By this, what might be said is refuted: "A certain knowledge of the contrary is inconceivable without it affecting the will; therefore, its lack of effect on the will reveals the non-realization of knowledge of this description."

The reason for the refutation: The mere realization of knowledge of the contrary does not necessitate the realization of the will based upon it. Rather, it is the knowledge that relates to the necessity of the act along with the soul's commitment to it, as passed regarding the denial of the deniers and their rejection of the truth despite their certainty of it. A similar case is the "act by attention" (الفعل بالعناية), such as the fall of someone standing on a high beam to the ground merely by the conception of falling; his knowledge that in falling is his certain destruction does not prevent it.

Some have answered the original objection by stating that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams عليهم‌السلام have specific obligations (تكاليف) for each of them. They must plunge into these perils, even if that would be for us "throwing oneself into destruction," which is forbidden. This is alluded to in some narrations.

Others answered that the knowledge which makes obligations binding (ينجز التكاليف) is the knowledge from ordinary methods; as for other than that, it is not binding. Both views can be oriented toward what has been previously explained.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ وَكَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَشَهِدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ — "Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has borne witness to its like and believed [in it], while you are disdainful...’" [Qarai: 46:10]. The pronouns in "it is" (كان), "in it" (به), and "its like" (مثله), according to what the context provides, refer to the Quran. His saying: "and a witness from the Children of Israel has borne witness..." etc., is conjoined to the condition and shares in its apodosis. What is meant by "the like of the Quran" is its like in terms of its content of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), which is the original Book of the Torah that was revealed to Moses عليه‌السلام. His saying: "and believed, while you are disdainful" means the mentioned Israelite witness believed after his testimony.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" [Qarai: 46:10]. This is a causal explanation for the omitted apodosis, indicating it. The apparent meaning is: "Are you not astray?" rather than what has been said: "Have you not wronged?" because the causal explanation by Allah's non-guidance of the wrongdoers (الظالمين) only suits their being astray (ضلالهم), not their wrongdoing, even though they are characterized by both descriptions.

The meaning is: Say to the polytheists: Inform me, if this Quran is from Allah while you have disbelieved in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has borne witness to the like of the gnosis in the Quran, and he believed while you were disdainful, are you not in error? For Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

The one who bore witness to its like and believed, according to some narrations, is Abdullah ibn Salam, one of the Jewish scholars. Based on this, the verse is Medinan, not Meccan, because he was among those who believed in Medina. The saying of some that the use of the past tense in His saying: "and a witness from the Children of Israel has borne witness to its like and believed" is because the occurrence is certain while the story takes place in the future, is absurd; for it does not suit the verse being in the context of an argument. The polytheists would not have accepted the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله truthfulness in what he informed them of regarding future matters.

Regarding the meaning of the verse, there are other opinions, including that the one meant by "he who bore witness to its like and believed" is Moses عليه‌السلام, who bore witness to the Torah and believed in it. They turned from the previous meaning to this one to maintain that the verse is Meccan, and that Abdullah ibn Salam only embraced Islam in Medina.

Against this: First, there is no evidence for the verse being Meccan; let the story be evidence for it being Medinan. Second, after making Moses the Interlocutor (الكليم) عليه‌السلام a peer to these uncouth polytheists, they are compared to him, and it is said in essence: Moses عليه‌السلام believed in the Book revealed to him, while you were disdainful of believing in the Quran. Its absurdity is manifest.

It has also been said that "the like" (المثل) in the verse means the thing itself, as has been said regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him" [Qarai: 42:11]. This is as far-fetched as the previous one.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْ كانَ خَيْراً ما سَبَقُونا إِلَيْهِ — "The faithless say of the faithful, ‘Had it been [any] good, they would not have taken the lead over us in [reaching] it’" [Qarai: 46:11]. It is said: the preposition "of" (لـ) in His saying: "of the faithful" (للذين آمنوا) is for causation, meaning "because of their faith," and it resolves to the meaning of "concerning." The pronouns in "it been" (كان) and "in it" (إليه) refer to the Quran in terms of believing in it.

The meaning is: The faithless said concerning the faithful — i.e., because of their faith — "Had faith in the Quran been good, they — i.e., the believers — would not have preceded us to it."

Some said: What is meant by "the faithful" is some of the believers, and by the pronoun returning to them in His saying "they would not have taken the lead over us" is the other part. The preposition "of" is related to "said." The meaning is: The faithless said to some of the believers, "Had it been good, the [other] part of the believers — who are absent — would not have preceded us to it." This is far from the context of the verse.

Others said: What is meant by "the faithful" is all the believers, but in His saying "they would not have taken the lead over us" (ما سبقونا) there is an apostrophe (التفات), and the original was "you would not have taken the lead over us." This is as far-fetched as the previous one; addressing those present in the third person is not an apostrophe at all.

His saying: وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا بِهِ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هذا إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ — "And since they could not be guided by it, they will say, ‘This is an ancient fiction’" [Qarai: 46:11]. The pronoun in "by it" (به) refers to the Quran, as does the demonstrative "this" (هذا). Fiction (الإفك) is fabrication. That is: since they were not guided by the Quran due to their disdain for believing in it, they — the faithless — will say: "This — i.e., the Quran — is an ancient fiction and fabrication." Their saying "This is an ancient fiction" is like their saying: أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — "myths of the ancients" [Qarai: 6:25].

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً وَهذا كِتابٌ مُصَدِّقٌ لِساناً عَرَبِيًّا — "Before it the Book of Moses was a guide and a mercy, and this is a Book which confirms it, in the Arabic language" [Qarai: 46:12]. It appears that His saying: "Before it..." etc., is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية). The meaning is: They will say "This is an ancient fiction" while the Book of Moses, being a guide (إماماً) and a mercy (رحمةً), was before it — i.e., before the Quran — and this Quran is a Book confirming it, being in the Arabic language, so that it may warn those who do wrong and is a good news for the virtuous. How then could it be a fiction?

The Torah being a "guide" and a "mercy" means it was such that the Children of Israel took it as an exemplar and followed it in their deeds, and it was a mercy for those who believed in it and followed it in the rectification of their souls.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا — "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah,’ and then remain steadfast" [Qarai: 46:13]. What is meant by their saying "Our Lord is Allah" is their acknowledgment and testimony to the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) to Allah, the Glorified, and His uniqueness in it. By their "steadfastness" (استقامتهم) is meant their firmness in what they testified to without deviation or swerving, and their commitment to its practical requirements.

His saying: فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِم| وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ — "there is no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" [Qarai: 46:13]. That is, there is no potential disliked thing before them that they fear of a potential punishment, nor a realized disliked thing that they grieve over of punishment or terror. For fear (الخوف) is only from a disliked thing possible of occurrence, and grief (الحزن) is from a disliked thing realized in occurrence. The "then" (فـ) in His saying: "there is no fear..." etc., is due to the implied meaning of a condition; for the speech is in the sense of: "Whoever says 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remains steadfast, then there is no fear..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, abiding in it forever, as a reward for what they used to do" [Qarai: 46:14]. What is meant by "inhabitants of paradise" (أصحاب الجنة) is their constant presence in it. His saying: "abiding in it forever" (خالدين فيها) is a circumstantial clause emphasizing the meaning of inhabitancy.

The meaning is: Those who said "Our Lord is Allah" and then remained steadfast are the constant inhabitants of paradise, while abiding in it forever, as a reward for the acts of obedience and devotions they used to perform in the world.

Discussion of Narrations

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu Ubaydah, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: ائْتُونِي بِكِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا أَوْ أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Bring me a scripture [revealed] before this, or some vestige of knowledge, if you are truthful." He said: "By 'the scripture' He meant the Torah and the Gospel, and by 'some vestige of knowledge' (أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ), He meant the knowledge of the successors (أوصياء) of the prophets."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ahmad, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh, through Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding أَوْ أَثارَةٍ مِنْ عِلْمٍ — "or some vestige of knowledge," he said: "The script (الخط)."

I say: Perhaps what is meant by "the script" is a written book inherited from the prophets or the scholars of the past. However, in some of what is narrated in the interpretation of His saying: "or some vestige of knowledge," it is said to be "beautiful script," and in another "excellence of script," which is foreign to the context of the argument in the verse.

In al-Uyun, in the chapter on the assembly of al-Rida with al-Ma'mun, from him عليه‌السلام: My father narrated to me from my grandfather, from his fathers, from al-Husayn ibn Ali عليه‌السلام, who said: The Muhajirun and the Ansar gathered before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah, you have expenses for your maintenance and for the delegations that come to you. These are our properties along with our blood; judge regarding them as a righteous one who is rewarded. Give what you wish and judge as you wish without any constraint."

He said: So Allah sent down to him the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين), who said: "O Muhammad, قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — 'Say, "I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives"'" [Qarai: 42:23], meaning that you should love my near kin after me. They went out, and the hypocrites said: "Nothing prompted the Messenger of Allah to leave what we offered him except to urge us toward his near kin after him. It is nothing but something he fabricated (افتراه) in his assembly." This was a grave saying from them.

So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down this verse: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَلا تَمْلِكُونَ لِي مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ كَفَى بِهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَهُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Say, ‘If I have fabricated it, you cannot avail me anything against Allah. He knows best what you are engaged in. He suffices as a witness between me and you, and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful’" [Qarai: 46:8]. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent for them and said: "Has anything happened?" They said: "Yes, by Allah, O Messenger of Allah, some of us said harsh words that we disliked." So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited the verse to them, and they wept, and their weeping intensified. Then Allah, the Exalted, sent down: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَيَعْفُوا عَنِ السَّيِّئاتِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ — "It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants, and excuses their misdeeds and knows what you do" [Qarai: 42:25].

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Dawud narrated in his Nasikh through Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَما أَدْرِي ما يُفْعَلُ بِي وَلا بِكُمْ — "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you," he said: It was abrogated by this verse in al-Fath — so he went out to the people and gave them the good news of that which was forgiven for him — his past and future sins.

A man from the believers said: "Congratulations to you, O Prophet of Allah! We now know what will be done with you, but what will be done with us?" So Allah sent down in Surah al-Ahzab: وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ فَضْلاً كَبِيراً — "And give the good news to the faithful that there shall be for them a great bounty from Allah" [Qarai: 33:47], and He said: لِيُدْخِلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَيُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَكانَ ذلِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً — "that He may admit the faithful men and women into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that He may acquit them of their misdeeds — that is a great triumph with Allah" [Qarai: 48:5]. Thus, Allah clarified what He would do with him and with them.

I say: The narration is not without issues:

Firstly: Because of what was previously explained in the interpretation of the verse — namely His saying: "nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you" — that it is foreign to the knowledge of the Unseen that comes through revelation, by explicit indication from the Quran. Thus, the knowledge of forgiveness through revelation is not negated by it such that the verse of Surah al-Fath would abrogate it.

Secondly: Because the apparent meaning of the narration is that the "sin" (الذنب) whose forgiveness is declared by the verse of Surah al-Fath is sin in the sense of violating a masterly command or prohibition. It will be explained in the interpretation of Surah al-Fath — if Allah wills — that "sin" in the verse carries a different meaning.

Thirdly: Because the verses indicating the entry of believers into Paradise are very numerous in both Meccan and Medinan Surahs. The two verses of Surah al-Ahzab do not indicate more than what the other verses indicate. Thus, there is no reason to specify them as indicating the entry of believers into Paradise and the encompassing of forgiveness for them. Moreover, Surah al-Ahzab was revealed a long time before Surah al-Fath.

In the same source, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it with a sound chain) narrated from Awf ibn Malik al-Ashja'i, who said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله set out and I was with him until we entered a Jewish synagogue on their festival day, and they disliked our entering upon them.

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to them: "Show me twelve men from among you who testify that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; Allah will then remove from every Jew under the canopy of heaven the wrath that is upon him." They remained silent, and none of them answered him. Then he repeated it to them, and none answered him. Then he did it a third time, and none answered him. He said: "You have refused. By Allah, I am the Gatherer (الحاشر), I am the Successor (العاقب), and I am the Follower (المقفي), whether you believe or deny."

Then he turned away and I was with him until we were about to leave. Suddenly, a man from behind him said: "Stay as you are, O Muhammad!" He approached, and that man said: "O assembly of Jews, what kind of man do you know me to be among you?" They said: "By Allah, we do not know among us a man more knowledgeable of the Book of Allah, nor more learned than you, nor than your father, nor than your grandfather." He said: "I testify by Allah that he is the Prophet whom you find in the Torah and the Gospel." They said: "You have lied!" Then they rebuked him and spoke evil. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "You have lied; your word will not be accepted from you."

We went out, and we were three: the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, myself, and Ibn Salam. Then Allah sent down: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ وَكَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَشَهِدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has borne witness to its like and believed [in it], while you are disdainful... Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people’" [Qarai: 46:10].

I say: Regarding the revelation of the verse concerning Abdullah ibn Salam, there are other narrations from the Sunni paths besides this one. However, the context of the verse, especially His saying: "from the Children of Israel" (مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ), does not suit the address being to the Children of Israel. Furthermore, the Gospel was counted in the narration among their books, but it is not among their books and the Jews do not believe in it.

In some narrations, the verse was revealed concerning Ibn Yamin, one of their scholars, when he testified and embraced Islam, and the Jews called him a liar. The previous objection remains as it was.

Ayahs

وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ كُرْهًا وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًا ۖ وَحَمْلُهُۥ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ ثَلَـٰثُونَ شَهْرًا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً قَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَصْلِحْ لِى فِى ذُرِّيَّتِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَإِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٥)
We have enjoined man to be kind to his parents. His mother has carried him in travail, and bore him in travail, and his gestation and weaning take thirty months. When he comes of age and reaches forty years, he says, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which please You, and invest my descendants with righteousness. Indeed I have turned to you in penitence, and I am one of the Muslims.’ (15)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَنَتَجَاوَزُ عَن سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَعْدَ ٱلصِّدْقِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (١٦)
Such are the ones from whom We accept the best of what they do, and overlook their misdeeds, [who will be] among the inhabitants of paradise—a true promise which they had been given. (16)
وَٱلَّذِى قَالَ لِوَٰلِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَّكُمَآ أَتَعِدَانِنِىٓ أَنْ أُخْرَجَ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلْقُرُونُ مِن قَبْلِى وَهُمَا يَسْتَغِيثَانِ ٱللَّهَ وَيْلَكَ ءَامِنْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ فَيَقُولُ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٧)
As for him who says to his parents, ‘Fie on you! Do you promise me that I shall be raised [from the dead] when generations have passed away before me?’ And they invoke Allah’s help [and say]: ‘Woe to you! Believe! Indeed Allah’s promise is true.’ But he says, ‘These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (17)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (١٨)
Such are the ones against whom the word has become due, along with the nations of jinn and humans that have passed away before them. They were the losers. (18)
وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ ۖ وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٩)
For everyone there are degrees [of merit] pertaining to what he has done: He will recompense them fully for their works, and they will not be wronged. (19)
وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّبَـٰتِكُمْ فِى حَيَاتِكُمُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهَا فَٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ (٢٠)
The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be told,] ‘You have exhausted your good things in the life of the world and enjoyed them. So today you will be requited with a humiliating punishment for your acting arrogantly in the earth unduly, and because you used to transgress.’ (20)

(Commentary)

Since He divided people in His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَبُشْرى لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ (that it may warn those who do wrong, and as good news for the virtuous) [46:12] into the unjust and the virtuous, and since it was indicated therein that for the unjust there is that which is feared and cautioned against, while for the virtuous there is that which gladdens a person and brings him good news, He followed that in this section of verses with a detailed discourse on the matter. He explains that people consist of a group who turn to Allah in repentance, submitting to Him—these are the ones whose best deeds are accepted and whose misdeeds are overlooked among the inhabitants of Paradise—and a group of losers against whom the word has become due among the nations of jinn and humans that have passed away before them.

He exemplified the first group with one who is a believer in Allah, submitting to Him, and dutiful to his parents, asking Allah to inspire him with gratitude for the blessings He has bestowed upon him and his parents, and to enable him to perform righteous deeds and to reform his progeny. He exemplified the second group with one who is undutiful to his parents; when they call him to faith in Allah and the Last Day, he rebukes them and considers such matters to be naught but the fables of the ancients.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ إِحْساناً (We have enjoined man to be kind to his parents) [46:15] to the end of the verse. The injunction (الوصية)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is to direct another toward that which they should act upon, coupled with an exhortation. Enjoining (التوصية) is the intensive form of the injunction (الوصية). The Exalted said: وَوَصَّى بِها إِبْراهِيمُ بَنِيهِ (Abraham enjoined this upon his sons) [2:132]. Thus, its second object, which it reaches via the preposition bi (بـ), is of the nature of actions. Therefore, the meaning of "enjoining regarding the parents" is enjoining an action related to them, which is being kind to them.

Accordingly, the implied structure of the speech is: "We have enjoined man regarding his parents, that he should treat them with kindness (إحسانا)."

Regarding the grammatical position of إِحْساناً, there are other opinions. Some say it is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) based on the word "enjoined" (وصينا) implying the meaning of "we commanded kindness" (أحسنا); thus the estimation is: "We enjoined man, commanding kindness toward them." Others say it is an attribute of a suppressed verbal noun (مصدر محذوف) with an implied possessive: "an injunction characterized by kindness" (إيصاء ذا إحسان). Others say it is an object of purpose (مفعول له), and the estimation is: "We enjoined him regarding them because of Our kindness toward them," among other things that have been said.

In any case, being dutiful to parents and treating them with kindness is among the general rulings legislated in all divine laws (الشرائع), as previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً (Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: that you shall not ascribe any partners to Him, and you shall be good to your parents’) [6:151]. For this reason, He said: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ (We have enjoined man), making it general for every human being.

Then He, the Glorified, followed this by referring to what his mother endured in carrying him, giving birth to him, and weaning him, as an indication of the basis for the ruling and to stir his emotions and arouse his innate instinct of mercy and compassion. He said: حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ كُرْهاً وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهاً وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً (His mother carried him with hardship and gave birth to him with hardship, and his gestation and weaning take thirty months) [46:15]. That is, his mother carried him with a carriage characterized by hardship (كره)—meaning difficulty—due to the weight involved in the pregnancy; and she gave birth to him with a delivery characterized by hardship, due to the pain of labor.

As for His saying: وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً (and his gestation and weaning take thirty months), it has taken the minimum period of pregnancy, which is six months, while the remaining two years until the completion of thirty months are the period of breastfeeding. The Exalted said: وَالْوالِداتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كامِلَيْنِ (Mothers shall suckle their children for two full years) [2:233], and He said: وَفِصالُهُ فِي عامَيْنِ (and his weaning is in two years) [31:14].

Weaning (الفصال) is the separation between the child and breastfeeding. The two years were made the timeframe for weaning with the consideration that it occurs at the end of breastfeeding and is only realized upon the completion of two years.

His saying: حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً (until when he comes of age and reaches forty years) [46:15]. Coming of age (بلوغ الأشد) is reaching a time in life when a person's faculties become firm and strong. The disagreement regarding the meaning of "coming of age" was previously cited in the commentary on His saying: وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً (When he came of age, We gave him judgment and knowledge) [12:22]. Reaching forty years is usually concomitant with the perfection of the intellect (العقل).

His saying: قالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلى والِدَيَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ (he says, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which please You’) [46:15]. Inspiration (الإيزاع) is divine prompting (الإلهام). This inspiration is not an inspiration of knowledge (إلهام علم) by which a person knows what his soul was naturally ignorant of—as in His saying: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها (and the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its Godwariness) [91:7-8]—rather, it is a practical inspiration (إلهام عملي) in the sense of an internal prompting and calling toward performing good, thanking for blessings, and in short, righteous deeds.

He left the "blessing" (النعمة) for which he asked to be inspired with gratitude as general; thus, it encompasses outward blessings such as life, sustenance, perception, and will, as well as inward blessings such as faith in Allah, submission (الإسلام), humility before Him, reliance upon Him (التوكل), and total entrustment to Him (التفويض). Therefore, in his saying: رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ (My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing), there is a request that He inspire him to praise Him by manifesting His blessing in word and deed. As for word, it is apparent; as for deed, it is by utilizing these blessings in a manner that demonstrates they belong to Allah, the Glorified, who bestowed them upon him, and are not from himself. This necessitates the manifestation of servitude (العبودية) and being owned (المملوكية) by this human being in both his speech and action.

The explanation of the blessing with His saying: الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلى والِدَيَ (with which You have blessed me and my parents) implies his gratitude on his own behalf for the blessings specific to him, and on behalf of his parents for what He bestowed upon them; thus, he is a remembering tongue for them after they are gone.

His saying: وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ (and that I may do righteous deeds which please You) is coordinated with his saying: أَنْ أَشْكُرَ (to give thanks). It is a request that completes the request for gratitude for blessings, for gratitude beautifies the outward aspect of deeds, while the righteousness (الصلاحية) that Allah, the Exalted, is pleased with beautifies their inward aspect and makes them sincere (تخلصها) for Allah, the Exalted.

His saying: وَأَصْلِحْ لِي فِي ذُرِّيَّتِي (and right my offspring for me) [46:15]. Reforming the offspring (الإصلاح في الذرية) is the creation of righteousness within them, which, from Allah the Glorified, is His granting them success (توفيق) for righteous deeds, leading to the reformation of their souls. Restricting the reformation with the phrase "for me" (لي) is to indicate that their reformation should be in a manner from which he benefits—that is, that his offspring should be dutiful and kind to him just as he was to his parents.

The sum of the supplication is a request that Allah inspire him to thank His blessing and perform righteous deeds, and that he be dutiful and kind to his parents, and that his offspring be the same toward him as he was toward his parents. It has been mentioned more than once that thanking His blessings, in its true sense, is for the servant to be sincere (خالصاً) for Allah; thus, the meaning of the supplication boils down to a request for the sincerity of the soul and the righteousness of action.

His saying: إِنِّي تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَإِنِّي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ (Indeed I have turned to You in penitence, and I am one of the Muslims) [46:15]. That is, those who submit the matter to You, such that You do not will anything except that they will it; rather, they do not will except what You have willed.

The sentence serves as a justification for the requests contained in the supplication. It becomes clear from the verse—since He mentioned the supplication and did not reject it, but rather supported it with what He promised in His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ (It is they from whom We accept...)—that when repentance (التوبة) and submission (الإسلام) to Allah, the Glorified, are combined in a servant, it results in His, the Exalted, inspiring him with that which makes the servant among the purified (المُخلَصين)—with a fathah on the lam—in essence, and the sincere (المُخلِصين)—with a kasrah on the lam—in action. As for the purification of the essence, it was alluded to just now; as for the sincerity of action, it is because an action is not fit for His, the Exalted, acceptance and being raised to Him unless it is sincere for His Noble Face. The Exalted said: أَلا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخالِصُ (Look! [Only] exclusive devotion is due to Allah) [39:3].

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَنَتَجاوَزُ عَنْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ فِي أَصْحابِ الْجَنَّةِ (It is they from whom We accept the best of what they have done, and We overlook their misdeeds, [among those who will be] among the inhabitants of paradise...) [46:16]. Acceptance (التقبل) is more emphatic than mere receiving (القبول). What is meant by "the best of what they have done" are their acts of obedience, including both obligatory and recommended acts, for these are what are accepted and received. As for permissible acts (المباحات), although they possess a certain goodness, they are not "accepted" in this specific sense. This was mentioned in Majma' al-Bayan, and it is a fine explanation, supported by the contrast between accepting the best of what they did and overlooking their misdeeds. It is as if it were said: their deeds consist of acts of obedience—obligatory and recommended—which are the best of their deeds, so We accept them; and misdeeds, which We overlook; while that which is neither an act of obedience nor a good deed has no status regarding acceptance or otherwise.

His saying: فِي أَصْحابِ الْجَنَّةِ (among the inhabitants of paradise) is connected to His saying: نَتَجاوَزُ (We overlook). That is, We overlook their misdeeds among the group of those whose misdeeds We overlook from the inhabitants of Paradise. Thus, it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun "from them" (عنهم).

His saying: وَعْدَ الصِّدْقِ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ (a true promise which they were given) [46:16]. That is, Allah promises them with this speech a true promise which they were being promised until this time through the tongues of the prophets and messengers. Or, it means that He fulfills for them, through this acceptance and overlooking on the Day of Resurrection, the true promise they were promised in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي قالَ لِوالِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَكُما أَتَعِدانِنِي أَنْ أُخْرَجَ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ الْقُرُونُ مِنْ قَبْلِي (As for him who says to his parents, ‘Fie on you! Do you promise me that I shall be raised [from the dead] when generations have passed away before me?’) [46:17]. When He mentioned the human being who turned to Allah in repentance, submitted to Him, and asked Him for purity, sincerity, dutifulness to parents, and the reformation of his children, He contrasted him with this human being who disbelieves in Allah, His Messenger, and the Return (المعاد), and who is undutiful to his parents when they call him to faith and warn him of the Return.

So His saying: وَالَّذِي قالَ لِوالِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَكُما (As for him who says to his parents, ‘Fie on you!’) is apparently a subject (مبتدأ) carrying a collective meaning, and its predicate (خبر) is His subsequent saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ (It is they...). "Fie" (أف) is a word of annoyance intended to express discontent and pain. أَتَعِدانِنِي أَنْ أُخْرَجَ (Do you promise me that I shall be raised?)—the interrogation is for rebuke. The meaning is: Do you promise me that I shall be brought out of my grave, brought to life, and presented for the reckoning? That is, do you promise me the Return? وَقَدْ خَلَتِ الْقُرُونُ مِنْ قَبْلِي (when generations have passed away before me)—that is, while the case is that past nations who lived before me have perished, and none of them have been brought back to life or resurrected.

This, in their view, is an argument for denying the Return. Its premise is that if there were such a thing as bringing to life and resurrection, some of those who have perished until now—who are beyond counting over long, endless ages—would have been brought back to life, yet there is no news of them nor any trace. They did not realize that if the past generations returned as they suggest, that would be a resurrection and bringing to life in this world, whereas what Allah, the Glorified, has promised is the resurrection for the afterlife and the rising for another realm (نشأة) other than this world.

His saying: وَهُما يَسْتَغِيثانِ اللهَ وَيْلَكَ آمِنْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌ (while they [the parents] implore Allah’s help [and say], ‘Woe to you! Believe! Indeed Allah’s promise is true’) [46:17]. Imploring help (الاستغاثة) is seeking aid from Allah. That is, while the parents are seeking from Allah that He aid them and help them in establishing the proof and inclining him toward faith, saying to him: "Woe to you! Believe in Allah and what His Messenger has brought, including His promise of the Return, for Allah's promise of the Return through His messengers is true."

From this, it appears that their intent in saying آمِنْ (Believe!) is the command to believe in Allah and His Messenger regarding what he brought from Allah, and their saying إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌ (Indeed Allah’s promise is true) refers to the Return. The justification for the command to believe in it is for the purpose of warning and instilling fear.

His saying: فَيَقُولُ ما هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ (But he says, ‘These are nothing but fables of the ancients’) [46:17]. The demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to the promise they mentioned and warned him about, or the sum total of what they were calling him toward. The meaning is: This human being says to his parents, "This promise you warn me with," or "this which you call me to, is nothing but the myths of the ancients"—meaning the early, primitive nations.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ (It is they against whom the word has become due...) [46:18]. Some discussion regarding this has already passed in the commentary on verse 25 of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah (Fussilat).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجاتٌ مِمَّا عَمِلُوا (For everyone there are degrees [of reward or punishment] depending on what they have done) [46:19] to the end of the verse. That is, for each of those mentioned—the righteous believers and the wicked disbelievers—there are different stations and ranks, ascending and descending. Thus, for Paradise there are degrees (درجات), and for the Fire there are depths (دركات).

This difference traces back to the differences within themselves, even if it manifests in their deeds. For this reason, He said: لِكُلٍّ دَرَجاتٌ مِمَّا عَمِلُوا (For everyone there are degrees depending on what they have done); so the degrees are for them, and their source is their deeds.

His saying: وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ (so that He may fully pay them for their deeds, and they will not be wronged) [46:19]. The lam is for purpose (للغاية), and the sentence is coordinated with another suppressed purpose or purposes that were not intended to be mentioned. It was made a purpose for His saying: لِكُلٍّ دَرَجاتٌ (For everyone there are degrees) because it carries the meaning of "We have made them into degrees." The meaning is: We made them into degrees for such-and-such reasons, and so that He may fully pay them for their deeds and they will not be wronged.

The meaning of "fully paying them for their deeds" (توفيتهم أعمالهم) is giving them the deeds themselves. Thus, the verse is among those indicating the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال). It has also been said that the speech implies a suppressed possessive, the estimation being: "so that He may fully pay them the rewards of their deeds."

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ (The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire...) [46:20]. "Exposing (عرض) water to the beast" or "to the beast" means placing it in its sight such that if it wishes, it may drink. "Exposing goods for sale" means placing them in a position where there is no barrier to the sale occurring.

Regarding His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ, it has been said that their being "exposed to the Fire" means their being punished in it, from the expression "so-and-so was exposed to the sword" when he was killed; this is a common metaphor.

However, this is challenged by His saying at the end of the surah: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِّ قالُوا بَلى وَرَبِّنا قالَ فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ (The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire [they will be asked], ‘Is this not real?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘So taste the punishment...’) [46:34]. This does not fit that interpretation so well, as the "tasting of the punishment" is made a consequence of the "exposure," implying they are distinct.

It has also been said that there is an inversion (قلب) in the verse, and the original meaning is "exposing the Fire to those who disbelieve." This is because for the meaning of "exposure" to be realized, the one to whom something is exposed must possess perception (شعور) of the thing exposed, and the Fire has no perception of those who disbelieve; rather, the case is the opposite. Thus, there is an inversion in the speech, and the intent is exposing the Fire to those who disbelieve.

Some commentators justified this by saying it is appropriate for the thing exposed to be brought to the one it is exposed to, as in our saying: "I exposed the water to the beast" and "I exposed the food to the guest." Since the case in exposing the Fire to those who disbelieve is the opposite—as they are the ones being driven to the Fire—the speech was inverted in consideration of this.

There is a problem with this view. As for the claim that the one to whom something is exposed must possess perception and awareness of the thing exposed so as to desire it or be repelled by it, while the Fire has no perception: firstly, this is rejected, as supported by the expression "I exposed the goods for sale," and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبَالِ (Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains) [33:72]. Secondly, we do not concede that the Fire of the Hereafter is devoid of perception. In authentic narrations (الأخبار الصحيحة), it is stated that Paradise and the Fire possess perception, and this is sensed in His saying: يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ (The day We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ and it will say, ‘Is there any more?’) [50:30], and other verses.

As for the claim that it is appropriate to move the thing exposed toward the one it is exposed to, we do not concede its necessity or its universality. It is refuted by His saying: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth) [33:72].

Furthermore, in His speech, the Exalted, there is that which indicates the bringing of the Fire to those who disbelieve, such as His saying: وَجِيءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ وَأَنَّى لَهُ الذِّكْرى (and hell is brought near on that day—on that day man will take admonition, but what will the admonition avail him?) [89:23].

The truth is that "exposure" (العرض)—which is the manifestation of the absence of any barrier to the connection of one thing with another—is a meaning that has a relation to both sides. It is possible to take either of them as the primary "one to whom it is exposed" and the other as the secondary "thing exposed." Sometimes the Fire is taken as the thing exposed to the disbelievers, with the consideration that there is no barrier—such as a righteous deed or intercession (شفاعة)—to prevent their entry into it, as in His saying: وَعَرَضْنا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَرْضاً (and We shall present hell to the faithless on that day) [18:100]. Other times, the disbelievers are taken as the ones exposed to the Fire, with the consideration that there is no barrier preventing the Fire from punishing them, as in His saying: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا (the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening) [40:46], and His saying: يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ (the faithless are exposed to the Fire).

Based on this, it is most likely that two exposures occur on the Day of Resurrection: the exposure of Hell to the disbelievers when it is made manifest to them, and then their exposure to Hell after the reckoning and the decisive judgment for their entry into it when they are driven toward it. The Exalted said: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً (The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs) [39:71].

His saying: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا وَاسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِها ([saying,] ‘You exhausted your good things in your worldly life and enjoyed them’) [46:20]. This is on the estimation of a suppressed "saying"; that is, it is said to them: "You exhausted..." etc. Good things (الطيبات) are those matters that suit the soul, agree with one's nature, and which a person finds pleasurable. "Exhausting the good things" (إذهاب الطيبات) is to finish them by fully consuming them. What is meant by "enjoying them" (الاستمتاع بها) is using them and benefiting from them for their own sake, not for the sake of the Hereafter or preparing for it.

The meaning is: It is said to them when they are exposed to the Fire: "You finished the good things you took pleasure in during your worldly life, and you enjoyed those good things, so nothing remains for you to take pleasure in during the Hereafter."

His saying: فَالْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَبِما كُنْتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ (So today you will be requited with a humiliating punishment for your acting arrogantly in the earth unduly, and for the transgressions you used to commit) [46:20]. This is a consequence of their exhausting the good things. The humiliating punishment (عذاب الهون) is the punishment that contains ignominy and disgrace.

The meaning is: So today you are requited with the punishment that contains ignominy and disgrace in exchange for your arrogance in the world against the truth, and in exchange for your transgression (فسقكم) and turning away from acts of obedience. These are two sins: one related to belief, which is arrogance against the truth, and the second related to action, which is transgression.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq, Abd ibn Humayd, and ibn al-Mundhir narrated via Qatadah from Abu Harb ibn Abi al-Aswad al-Du'ali, who said: "A woman who gave birth at six months was brought to Umar. He consulted the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, and Ali عليه السلام said: 'There is no stoning for her. Do you not see that He says: وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً (and his gestation and weaning take thirty months), and He says: وَفِصالُهُ فِي عامَيْنِ (and his weaning is in two years)?
