Thus, the pregnancy here was six months.' So Umar left her." He said: "Then it reached us that she gave birth to another at six months."

I say: Al-Mufid narrated the story in al-Irshad.

In the same source, ibn al-Mundhir and ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ba'jah ibn Abdillah al-Juhani, who said: "A man from among us married a woman from Juhaynah. She gave birth to a full-term child at six months. Her husband went to Uthman ibn Affan, who ordered her to be stoned. This reached Ali عليه السلام, so he came to him and said: 'What are you doing?' He replied: 'She gave birth to a full-term child at six months; can such a thing be?' Ali عليه السلام said: 'Have you not heard Allah, the Exalted, saying: وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً (and his gestation and weaning take thirty months), and He said: حَوْلَيْنِ كامِلَيْنِ (two full years)? How much do you find remains except six months?'"

"Uthman said: 'By Allah, I did not realize this. Bring the woman to me!' They found that she had already been dealt with. Among her words to her sister was: 'Do not grieve, for by Allah, no one has ever uncovered my private parts except him.' He said: 'The boy grew up afterward and the man recognized him, for he was the person most resembling him.' He said: 'I saw the man afterward, his limbs falling off one by one on his bed.'"

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah عليه السلام, who said: "My father asked him while I was present about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ (until when he comes of age). He said: 'It is the nocturnal emission (الاحتلام).'"

In al-Khisal, from Abu Basir, who said: "Abu Abdillah عليه السلام said: 'When a servant reaches thirty-three years, he has come of age (بلغ أشده). When he reaches forty years, he has reached his peak (منتهاه). When he enters his forty-first year, he is in decline. It is fitting for the one of fifty years to be like one who is in the throes of death.'"

I say: The narration is not devoid of an indication that "coming of age" (بلوغ الأشد) is something that varies in degrees. Thus, the nocturnal emission—which is usually at sixteen—is the first degree of it, and thirty-three—which is after another sixteen years have passed—is the second degree. Some other narrations were previously mentioned regarding the similar verse in Surah Yusuf.

Know that there are narrations regarding the verse that apply it to al-Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام and his birth at six months; these are of the nature of "esoteric application" (الجري).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ibn Abi Hatim and ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abdullah, who said: "I was in the mosque when Marwan delivered a sermon and said: 'Indeed, Allah has shown the Commander of the Faithful a good opinion regarding Yazid; if he appoints him as successor, then Abu Bakr and Umar also appointed successors.' Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr said: 'Is it a Heraclian [dynasty]? By Allah, Abu Bakr did not give it to any of his children, nor anyone from his household. Mu'awiyah has only done it out of mercy and honor for his son.'"

"Marwan said: 'Are you not the one who said to his parents: "Fie on you both"?' Abd al-Rahman replied: 'Are you not the son of the accursed one—whom the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم cursed, along with your father?'"

He said: "Aishah heard this and said: 'O Marwan! Are you the one who said such-and-such to Abd al-Rahman? You have lied, by Allah! It was not revealed concerning him. It was revealed concerning so-and-so, son of so-and-so.'"

In the same source, ibn Jarir narrated from ibn Abbas regarding the one who said to his parents: أُفٍّ لَكُما (Fie on you both) [46:17], he said: "This was a son of Abu Bakr." I say: This was also narrated from Qatadah and al-Suddi. The story of Marwan's narration and Aishah's refutation of him is famous. The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani said after rejecting Marwan's narration: "Some, like al-Suhayli in al-A'lam, agreed with Marwan in the claim that it was revealed concerning Abd al-Rahman. Even if that were conceded, there is no meaning in shaming him, especially from Marwan, for the man [Abd al-Rahman] embraced Islam and was among the most virtuous and heroic of the companions. He had great efforts in Islam on the Day of Yamamah and elsewhere, and Islam wipes away what preceded it. Thus, when a disbeliever embraces Islam, it is not fitting to shame him for what he used to say." End quote.

The problem with this is that if the narrations were authentic, there would be no escape from the explicit testimony of the verse against him in His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ (It is they against whom the word has become due...) to His saying: إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا خاسِرِينَ (Indeed they were losers) [46:18]. Nothing used to defend him would be of avail.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (The day when the faithless are exposed...) to His saying: وَاسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِها (and enjoyed them) [46:20], he said: "You ate, drank, and rode; and it is concerning the sons of so-and-so. فَالْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ (So today you will be requited with a humiliating punishment), he said: 'Thirst.'"

In al-Mahasin, with its chain to ibn al-Qaddah from Abu Abdillah عليه السلام from his fathers عليهم السلام, who said: "A dish of khabis (a type of sweet) was brought to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, but he refused to eat it. It was said: 'Do you forbid it?' He replied: 'No, but I dislike that my soul should long for it.' Then he recited the verse: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّباتِكُمْ فِي حَياتِكُمُ الدُّنْيا (You exhausted your good things in your worldly life) [46:20]."

In al-Majma', regarding the verse, it was narrated in a hadith that Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "I sought permission to enter upon the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. I entered upon him in the upper room of the mother of Ibrahim. He was lying on a reed mat, part of which was on the dust. Under his head was a pillow stuffed with palm fiber. I greeted him and then sat down and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! You are the Prophet of Allah, His chosen one, and the best of His creation, yet Khosrow and Caesar are upon thrones of gold and carpets of silk and brocade!' The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: 'Those are a people whose good things were hastened for them, and they are soon to be cut off. Our good things have been delayed for us.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through multiple chains from him.

Ayahs

۞ وَٱذْكُرْ أَخَا عَادٍ إِذْ أَنذَرَ قَوْمَهُۥ بِٱلْأَحْقَافِ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلنُّذُرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦٓ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٢١)
And mention [Hud] the brother of ‘Ad, when he warned his people at Ahqaf—and warners have passed away before and after him—saying, ‘Do not worship anyone but Allah. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (21)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَأْفِكَنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٢)
They said, ‘Have you come to turn us away from our gods? Then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful. (22)
قَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ (٢٣)
He said, ‘The knowledge [of when that threat will be carried out] is with Allah alone. I communicate to you what I have been sent with. But I see that you are a senseless lot.’ (23)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عَارِضًا مُّسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا عَارِضٌ مُّمْطِرُنَا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ مَا ٱسْتَعْجَلْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ رِيحٌ فِيهَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٤)
When they saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they said, ‘This cloud brings us rain.’ ‘No, it is what you sought to hasten: a hurricane carrying a painful punishment, (24)
تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَىْءٍۭ بِأَمْرِ رَبِّهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ لَا يُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٢٥)
destroying everything by its Lord’s command.’ So they became such that nothing could be seen except their dwellings. Thus do We requite the guilty lot. (25)
وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِيمَآ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِيهِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ سَمْعًا وَأَبْصَـٰرًا وَأَفْـِٔدَةً فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَلَآ أَفْـِٔدَتُهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ إِذْ كَانُوا۟ يَجْحَدُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٢٦)
Certainly We had granted them power in respects that We have not granted you, and We had vested them with hearing and sight and hearts. But neither their hearing availed them in any way nor did their sight, nor their hearts when they used to impugn the signs of Allah. So they were besieged by what they used to deride. (26)
وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا مَا حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَصَرَّفْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٧)
Certainly We have destroyed the towns that were around you, and We have variously paraphrased the signs so that they may come back. (27)
فَلَوْلَا نَصَرَهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ قُرْبَانًا ءَالِهَةًۢ ۖ بَلْ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٨)
So why did not those [fake deities] help them whom they had taken as gods besides Allah, as a means of nearness [to Him]? Indeed, they forsook them; that was their lie and what they used to fabricate. (28)

(Commentary)

When the people were divided into two groups and the discourse reached the point of warning (الإنذار), it was followed by an allusion to two stories: the story of the people of ‘Ad and their destruction—along with a reference to the destruction of the townships around Mecca—and the story of the faith of a group of jinn whom Allah directed toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who then listened to the Quran, believed, and returned to their people as warners (منذرين). These two stories were cited so that whoever among them wished to take heed might do so. These quoted verses contain the first of the two stories.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ أَخا عادٍ إِذْ أَنْذَرَ قَوْمَهُ بِالْأَحْقافِ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ النُّذُرُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ “And mention the brother of ‘Ad, when he warned his people at Ahqaf—and warners have passed away before him and after him...” (46:21). The "brother" of a people is one related to them through the father. What is meant by the "brother of ‘Ad" is the Prophet Hud (عليه‌السلام). Al-Ahqaf (الأحقاف) was the dwelling place of the people of ‘Ad; it is certain that it was in the south of the Arabian Peninsula, though no trace of them remains today. Scholars have differed as to its exact location: it is said to be a valley between Oman and Mahrah; it is said to be the sands between Oman and Hadramaut; it is said to be the sands overlooking the sea at Ash-Shihr in the land of Yemen; and other opinions have been offered.

His saying: وَقَدْ خَلَتِ النُّذُرُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ “and warners (النذر) have passed away before him and after him.” Al-Nudhur (النذر) is the plural of nadhir (نذير), meaning a messenger (الرسول), as indicated by the context. As for the generalization made by some that "warners" includes both messengers and their deputies among the scholars, it is misplaced here.

They have interpreted "before him" (مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ) as those who came before him, and "after him" (مِنْ خَلْفِهِ) as those who came after him. The reverse is also possible: that "before him" refers to those in his own time, and "after him" refers to those who preceded him. However, according to the first interpretation, the passing of warners before and after him is more likely an allusion to his coming to them and warning them during an interval (فترة) between messengers.

His saying: أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ “saying, ‘Worship no one but Allah’” (46:21) is an explanation of the warning (الإنذار). It contains an indication that the foundation of his religion, to which all its details return, is monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts.

His saying: إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ “Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day” (46:21) is the justification for calling them to monotheism. It appears that what is meant by the "tremendous day" is the day of the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), not the Day of Resurrection. This is evidenced by their subsequent saying: فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا “so bring us what you threaten us with” (46:22) and His saying: بَلْ هُوَ مَا اسْتَعْجَلْتُمْ بِهِ “Rather it is what you sought to hasten” (46:24). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِتَأْفِكَنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا “They said, ‘Have you come to turn us away from our gods...’” (46:22). This was the people's response to his warning. His saying: لِتَأْفِكَنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا employs the word al-ifk (الإفك) — falsehood and fabrication — while implying the meaning of "turning away" (الصرف). The meaning is: They said, "Have you come to turn us away from our gods through falsehood and fabrication?"

His saying: فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ “so bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful” (46:22) was a command intended to show his inability, based on their assumption that he (عليه‌السلام) was lying in his claims and fabricating in his warning.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ “He said, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I communicate to you what I have been sent with...’” (46:23). This was Hud's response to them, refuting their claim. His saying: إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ restricts the knowledge of when the punishment will descend to Him, the Exalted, because it belongs to the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — the reality of which is known to none but Allah, majestic is His status. This is an allusion to the fact that he (عليه‌السلام) had no knowledge of what it is, how it is, or when it will be. Therefore, he followed it by saying: وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ meaning: "That which I have been burdened with and sent to you with is what I communicate to you. I have no knowledge of the punishment I was commanded to warn you of—what it is, how it is, or when it is—nor do I have power over it."

His saying: وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ “but I see that you are an ignorant people” (46:23) is a transition (إضراب) from what the speech implies regarding his denial of knowledge for himself. The meaning is: "I have no knowledge of the punishment you seek to hasten, but I see you as a people who are ignorant (تجهلون), unable to distinguish what benefits you from what harms you, or your good from your evil, when you reject the call of Allah, deny His signs, and mock the punishment He threatens you with."

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عارِضاً مُسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قالُوا هذا عارِضٌ مُمْطِرُنا “When they saw it as a cloud crossing toward their valleys, they said, ‘This is a cloud that will bring us rain’...” (46:24). This describes the descent of the punishment upon them at its first appearance.

The ‘arid (العارض) is the cloud that appears on the horizon and then covers the sky. It is a description of the punishment to which the pronoun in "they saw it" (رَأَوْهُ) refers, as understood from the context. His saying: مُسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ “crossing toward their valleys” is another description of it. Al-awdiyah (الأودية) is the plural of wadi (الوادي). His saying: قالُوا هذا عارِضٌ مُمْطِرُنا means they rejoiced, assuming it was a passing cloud that would bring them rain, so they said: "This which we observe is a passing cloud that will rain upon us."

His saying: بَلْ هُوَ مَا اسْتَعْجَلْتُمْ بِهِ رِيحٌ فِيها عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ “Rather it is what you sought to hasten: a wind wherein is a painful punishment” (46:24) is a refutation of their saying: هذا عارِضٌ مُمْطِرُنا. It shifts from their claim to clarify the reality. He first explained, by way of sarcasm (التهكم), that it is the very punishment they sought to hasten when they said: فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ. He then further clarified it by saying: رِيحٌ فِيها عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ “a wind wherein is a painful punishment.”

This speech is from Allah, the Exalted, though it has been said that it is the speech of the Prophet Hud (عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ بِأَمْرِ رَبِّها فَأَصْبَحُوا لا يُرى إِلَّا مَساكِنُهُمْ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ “It destroys everything by the command of its Lord. So they became such that nothing could be seen except their dwellings. Thus do We let the guilty people be requited” (46:25). Al-tadmin (التدمير) means destruction (الإهلاك). Although its attachment to "everything" implies universal destruction, the context restricts it to things like humans, livestock, and property. Thus, the meaning is: that wind was a wind that destroyed everything it passed over of humans, livestock, and wealth.

His saying: فَأَصْبَحُوا لا يُرى إِلَّا مَساكِنُهُمْ is an explanation of the result of the punishment's descent. His saying: كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ provides a universal principle (ضابط كلي) regarding the requital of the guilty by likening the universal to the specific instance used as an example; the comparison is in the intensity. That is: "Our sunnah (سنتنا) in requiting the guilty is of this manner of intensity that We have narrated." This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذا أَخَذَ الْقُرى وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ “Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the townships while they are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe” (11:102).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِيما إِنْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِيهِ “We had certainly established them in respects in which We have not established you...” (46:26). This is an admonition to the disbelievers of Mecca derived from the story.

Al-tamkin (التمكين) is to settle and establish something in a place. It is an allusion to granting power and the capacity (الاستطاعة) for disposal. The word ma (ما) in fima (فيما) is either a relative pronoun (موصولة) or a descriptive one (موصوفة), and in (إِنْ) is negating (نافية). The meaning is: "We certainly placed the people of Hud in that which—or in something which—We have not established you in, O disbelievers of Mecca and those who follow you, in terms of vastness of stature, physical strength, severe might, and national power."

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ سَمْعاً وَأَبْصاراً وَأَفْئِدَةً “and We had given them hearing, sight, and hearts” (46:26) means: "We equipped them with that by which they perceive what benefits them and what harms them—which is hearing and sight—and that by which they distinguish what benefits them from what harms them, so they might devise means to acquire benefit and repel harm through their power, just as you have these things."

His saying: فَما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلا أَبْصارُهُمْ وَلا أَفْئِدَتُهُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِذْ كانُوا يَجْحَدُونَ بِآياتِ اللهِ “but neither their hearing, nor their sight, nor their hearts availed them anything, since they used to deny the signs of Allah” (46:26). The ma (ما) in fa-ma aghna (فَما أَغْنى) is negating, not interrogative. Idh (إِذْ) is an adverbial particle (ظرف) related to the negation in His saying: fa-ma aghna.

The summary of the meaning is: They possessed an establishment (التمكن) that you do not have, and they had the tools of perception and discernment by which a human devises means to repel calamities and guard against destructive, annihilating events. Yet, these senses and hearts availed them nothing and did not benefit them at all when they denied the signs of Allah. So, what makes you feel secure from the punishment of Allah while you are deniers of Allah's signs?

It has also been said that the meaning of the verse is: "We established them in that which—or in something which—We have not established you in, regarding strength and capacity, and We gave them hearing, sight, and hearts so they might use them for what they were created for: to hear the word of truth, witness the signs of monotheism, take heed by reflecting on lessons, and deduce the Origin and the Return through sound reasoning. Yet, neither their hearing, nor their sight, nor their hearts availed them anything because they did not use them for what leads to the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified." This meaning, however, may be less suited to the context than the one we previously mentioned.

Scholars have allowed various views regarding the individual words of the verse which we have not cited due to their lack of utility.

It has been previously mentioned in similar instances to His saying: سَمْعاً وَأَبْصاراً وَأَفْئِدَةً that the singular form of "hearing" (السمع)—while the plural is intended—is due to its being a verbal noun (مصدر) in origin, similar to "guest" (الضيف), "offering" (القربان), and "ritually impure" (الجنب). The Exalted said: ضَيْفِ إِبْراهِيمَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ “the honored guests (ضيف) of Abraham” (51:24); He said: إِذْ قَرَّبا قُرْباناً “when they both offered an offering (قربانا)” (5:27); and He said: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ جُنُباً “and if you are ritually impure (جنبا)” (5:6).

His saying: وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ “and they were beset by what they used to mock” (46:26) is coordinated (عطف) with His saying: فَما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنا ما حَوْلَكُمْ مِنَ الْقُرى “We have certainly destroyed the townships around you...” (46:27) is a warning reminder branching from the admonition in His saying: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ etc. It is coordinated with it as indicated by the context, not with His saying: وَاذْكُرْ أَخا عادٍ.

His saying: وَصَرَّفْنَا الْآياتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ “and We have varied the signs so that they may come back” (46:27) means: "We diversified the various signs—including miracles with which We supported the prophets, revelation We sent down to them, blessings We provided them so they might remember, and afflictions We tested them with so they might repent and turn away from their wrongdoing—so that they might return from the worship of other than Allah, the Glorified, to His worship."

The pronoun in "so that they (لَعَلَّهُمْ) may come back" refers to the townships (القرى), meaning the people of the townships.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لا نَصَرَهُمُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ قُرْباناً آلِهَةً “Why did not those whom they had taken for gods besides Allah as a means of nearness help them?” (46:28). The apparent context is that "gods" (آلِهَةً) is the second object of "they had taken" (اتَّخَذُوا), and its first object is the pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun. قربانا (qurbanan) means that by which one seeks nearness. The speech is intended as sarcasm (التهكم). The meaning is: "Why then did those whom they took as gods not help them, while they were being used as a means of nearness to Allah?"—as they used to say: ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى “We do not worship them except that they may bring us near to Allah in station” (39:3).

His saying: بَلْ ضَلُّوا عَنْهُمْ “Rather they deserted them” (46:28) means the gods vanished from the people of the townships, and the link of divinity and servitude—which they had claimed and by which they hoped to be helped during hardships and calamities—was severed. Their "straying" (الضلال) from them is an allusion to the futility of their claim.

His saying: وَذلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ “That was their falsehood and what they used to fabricate” (46:28) consists of a subject and a predicate. The "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the vanishing of their gods. What is meant by "falsehood" (الإفك) is the effect of the falsehood, or there is an implied omitted word. The ma (ما) is a verbal noun particle (مصدرية). The meaning is: "That vanishing was the effect of their falsehood and their fabrication."

It is also possible for the speech to be taken as it is, without an implied word or metaphor, with "that" referring to their destruction after the variation of the signs and the vanishing of their gods at that time. The summary of the meaning is: "This which We have mentioned of their end is the reality of their claim that the gods would intercede for them and bring them near to Allah—the claim which they falsified and fabricated." The speech is intended as sarcasm.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَآ إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنصِتُوا۟ ۖ فَلَمَّا قُضِىَ وَلَّوْا۟ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (٢٩)
When We dispatched toward you a team of jinn listening to the Quran, when they were in its presence, they said, ‘Be silent!’ When it was finished, they went back to their people as warners. (29)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا كِتَـٰبًا أُنزِلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِلَىٰ طَرِيقٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٣٠)
They said, ‘O our people! Indeed, we have heard a Book, which has been sent down after Moses, confirming what was before it. It guides to the truth and to a straight path. (30)
يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ أَجِيبُوا۟ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُجِرْكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣١)
O our people! Respond to Allah’s summoner and have faith in Him. He will forgive you some of your sins and shelter you from a painful punishment.’ (31)
وَمَن لَّا يُجِبْ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَيْسَ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءُ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٢)
Those who do not respond to Allah’s summoner cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and they will not find any protectors besides Him. They are in manifest error. (32)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَمْ يَعْىَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٣)
Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and [who] was not exhausted by their creation, is able to revive the dead? Yes, indeed He has power over all things. (33)
وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٤)
The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [He will say,] ‘Is this not a fact?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve.’ (34)
فَٱصْبِرْ كَمَا صَبَرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعَزْمِ مِنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَلَا تَسْتَعْجِل لَّهُمْ ۚ كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ مَا يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّن نَّهَارٍۭ ۚ بَلَـٰغٌ ۚ فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٣٥)
So be patient just as the resolute among the apostles were patient, and do not seek to hasten [the punishment] for them. The day when they see what they are promised, [it will be] as though they had remained [in the world] only an hour of a day. This is a proclamation. So shall anyone be destroyed except the transgressing lot? (35)

(Commentary)

This is the second story following the story of ‘Ad, presented so that his [the Prophet’s] people (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) might take heed, if they are to take heed at all.

In it is a rebuke for the people, as they disbelieved in him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and in his Book revealed in their own tongue, while they knew it to be a miraculous sign (آية معجزة). Despite this, and despite their sharing the same human nature with him, the Jinn believed in the Quran when they listened to it and returned to their people as warners.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ صَرَفْنا إِلَيْكَ نَفَراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْآنَ “And when We turned toward you a team of jinn to listen to the Quran” (46:29) until the end of the verse. Turning (الصرف) is to redirect something from one state to another or from one place to another. A team (النفر)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is a group of men capable of "nearing" (النفر) [for battle or travel]; it is a collective noun applied to a group of more than three among men, women, humans, and also Jinn, as in this verse. The phrase “listening to the Quran” (يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْآنَ) is an adjective describing the "team" (نفر). The meaning is: And remember when We directed toward you a group of Jinn to listen to the Quran.

His saying: فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قالُوا أَنْصِتُوا “When they were in its presence, they said, ‘Be silent!’” (46:29). The pronoun in “were in its presence” (حَضَرُوهُ) refers to the Quran, alluding to the act of its recitation. Silence (الإنصات) is remaining quiet for the purpose of listening. That is, when they attended the reading and recitation of the Quran, they said—meaning to one another: "Be silent so that we may listen with due attention."

His saying: فَلَمَّا قُضِيَ وَلَّوْا إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ مُنْذِرِينَ “And when it was finished, they returned to their people as warners” (46:29). The pronoun in “it was finished” (قُضِيَ) refers to the Quran in terms of its reading and recitation. Returning (التولية) means departing. “As warners” (مُنْذِرِينَ) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the plural pronoun in “they returned” (وَلَّوْا). That is, when the recitation was completed and finished, they departed to their people in the state of being warners, cautioning them against the punishment of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا قَوْمَنا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا كِتاباً أُنْزِلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مُوسى مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ “They said, ‘O our people! Indeed we have heard a Book revealed after Moses, confirming what was before it’” (46:30) etc. This is a narration of their call to their people and their warning to them. The "Book revealed after Moses" refers to the Quran. In this speech, there is an indication—rather, a proof—that they were believers in Moses (عليه‌السلام) and his Book. The Quran’s "confirming what was before it" refers to its confirmation of the Torah or all previous divine scriptures.

His saying: يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ “guiding to the truth and to a straight path” (46:30). That is, it guides whoever follows it to the path of truth (الحق) and to a straight path (طريق مستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — such that those who traverse it do not stray from the truth in belief or action.

His saying, the Exalted: يا قَوْمَنا أَجِيبُوا داعِيَ اللهِ وَآمِنُوا بِهِ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُجِرْكُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ “O our people! Respond to Allah’s inviter and believe in Him, so that He may forgive you some of your sins and deliver you from a painful punishment” (46:31). The "inviter of Allah" (داعي الله) refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Exalted said: قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ “Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight’” (12:108). It has been said that it refers to what they heard of the Quran, but this is far-fetched.

It appears that the word "some" (مِنْ) in “forgive you some of your sins” (يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). The meaning is the forgiveness of some sins, namely those they committed before attaining faith (الإيمان). The Exalted said: إِنْ يَنْتَهُوا يُغْفَرْ لَهُمْ ما قَدْ سَلَفَ “if they desist, what is past shall be forgiven them” (8:38).

It has also been said that this "some" refers to the rights of Allah, the Glorified, for they are forgiven through repentance (التوبة) and faith, whereas the rights of people are not forgiven through repentance alone. This has been refuted by the principle that "Islam erases what preceded it."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ لا يُجِبْ داعِيَ اللهِ فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَيْسَ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءُ “Whosoever does not respond to Allah’s inviter cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and he will have no protectors besides Him” (46:32) etc. That is, whoever does not believe in Allah’s inviter cannot frustrate Allah on earth by rejecting His call, nor will he have any guardians (أولياء) besides Allah to help or support him in that. The gist is: whoever does not respond to Allah’s inviter in his call has only wronged himself; he cannot frustrate Allah thereby, neither independently nor through the help of any guardians who might assist him, for he has no guardians besides Allah. Therefore, He concluded the speech by saying: أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ “They are in manifest error” (46:32).

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَلَمْ يَعْيَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقادِرٍ “Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not exhausted by their creation, is able...” (46:33) etc. This verse and those following it until the end of the Surah are connected to what preceded in His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ “The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire: ‘You exhausted...’” (46:20) etc. In these verses is the completion of the discourse regarding that which is the subject of warning in this Surah, namely the Resurrection (المعاد) and the return to Allah, the Exalted, as we indicated in the preceding commentary.

"Seeing" (الرؤية) here means knowledge derived from insight. Exhaustion (العي) means incapacity and fatigue; the former is more eloquent according to what has been said. The particle bi in “is able” (بِقادِرٍ) is extra (زائدة), occurring in a position that carries a scent of negation, as if it were said: "Is Allah not able?"

The meaning is: Do they not know that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not incapable of their creation, nor fatigued by it, is able to give life to the dead—and He, the Exalted, is the Originator of the existence and life of everything? Yes, He is able, for He has power over all things. We have clarified this argument several times previously.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِ “The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire: ‘Is this not the truth?’” (46:34) until the end of the verse. This is a reinforcement of the argument mentioned in the previous verse by informing of what will happen to the deniers of the Resurrection on the Day of Judgment. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ كَما صَبَرَ أُولُوا الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ وَلا تَسْتَعْجِلْ لَهُمْ “So be patient just as the resolute among the apostles were patient, and do not seek to hasten it for them” (46:35) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) based on the reality of the Resurrection as established by rational proof and informed by Allah, the Glorified, and the negation of any doubt regarding it.

The meaning is: So be patient with the denial of these disbelievers and their lack of faith in that Day, just as the resolute (أولوا العزم) among the messengers were patient, and do not seek to hasten the punishment for them, for they shall meet that Day with its punishment, and that Day is not far from them, even if they consider it far.

His saying: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ “The day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] but for an hour of a day” (46:35). This is an explanation of the proximity of that Day to them and to their worldly life, by informing of their state when they witness that Day. For when they see what they were promised of that Day and the punishment prepared for them therein, their state will be like that of one who tarried on earth for only an hour of a day.

His saying: بَلاغٌ فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْفاسِقُونَ “[This is] a delivery. So shall any be destroyed except the transgressing lot?” (46:35). That is, this Quran, with the clarification it contains, is a delivery (تبليغ) from Allah through the path of prophethood. Shall any be destroyed by this destruction which Allah has conveyed except the transgressing people (القوم الفاسقون) who have departed from the garment of servitude?

In this verse, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient as the resolute (أولوا العزم) among the messengers were patient. In this is an allusion that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is one of them, so let him be patient with a patience like theirs. The meaning of "resolve" (العزم) here is either patience (الصبر)—as some have said, based on His saying: وَلَمَنْ صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ “But whoever is patient and forgives—that is indeed the course of resolute conduct” (42:43)—or it is the resolve to fulfill the covenant (الميثاق) taken from the prophets, as alluded to in His saying: وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً “Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any resoluteness in him” (20:115), or "resolve" in the sense of "determination" (العزيمة), which refers to the Law (الحكم) and the Sharia (الشريعة).

According to the third meaning—which is the truth mentioned in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام)—they are five: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم وعليهم), based on His saying: شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى “He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus” (42:13). The explanation of the meaning of this verse has already passed.

It is narrated from some exegetes that all messengers are "resolute" (أولوا العزم), taking the phrase “among the apostles” (مِنَ الرُّسُلِ) as an explanatory phrase for "the resolute" in His saying: “the resolute among the apostles” (أُولُوا الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ). Others say they are the eighteen messengers mentioned in Surah al-An'am (verses 83–90), because the Exalted said after mentioning them: فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ “so follow their guidance” (6:90).

The refutation of this is that the Exalted said after counting them: وَمِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِخْوانِهِمْ “and from among their fathers, their descendants and their brethren” (6:87), then He said: “so follow their guidance,” and He did not say that immediately after counting them without interruption.

Some say they are nine: Noah, Abraham, the Sacrificed One [Ishmael], Jacob, Joseph, Job, Moses, David, and Jesus. Others say they are seven: Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, David, Solomon, and Jesus. Others say they are six, being those who were commanded to fight: Noah, Hud, Salih, Moses, David, and Solomon. Some mention that the six are Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, and Job. Some say they are five: Noah, Hud, Abraham, Shu'ayb, and Moses. Some say they are four: Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. Others mention that the four are Noah, Abraham, Hud, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم وعليهم أجمعين).

These opinions range between those for which no evidence is provided at all, and those for which evidence is provided that carries no proof. For this reason, we have overlooked citing them further. Some discussion regarding the resolute among the messengers (أولي العزم من الرسل) has already passed in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of this book; refer to it if you wish.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ صَرَفْنا إِلَيْكَ نَفَراً مِنَ الْجِنِ “And when We turned toward you a team of jinn” (46:29) etc., the reason for the revelation of these verses was that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out from Mecca to the market of ‘Ukaz, accompanied by Zayd ibn Harithah, calling the people to Islam. No one responded to him, nor did he find anyone to accept him, so he returned toward Mecca.

When he reached a place called Wadi Majannah (1), he performed the night prayer (تهجد) with the Quran in the middle of the night. A group of Jinn passed by him, and when they heard the recitation of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), they listened to him. When they heard his Quran, they said to one another: أَنْصِتُوا “Be silent!” (46:29), meaning keep quiet. فَلَمَّا قُضِيَ “And when it was finished” (46:29), meaning when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) finished the Quran, وَلَّوْا إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ مُنْذِرِينَ قالُوا يا قَوْمَنا “they returned to their people as warners. They said, ‘O our people!’” (46:29-30) until the end of the verses.

They came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), embraced Islam, and believed. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) taught them the laws of Islam. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): قُلْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِنَ الْجِنِ “Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that a team of jinn listened’” (72:1), the entire Surah. Thus Allah narrated their speech. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) appointed a leader over them from among themselves, and they used to return to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at all times. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) then commanded the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) to teach them and give them understanding of the religion (يفقههم). Among them are believers, disbelievers, and Nasibis (ناصبون) — those who harbor enmity toward the Ahl al-Bayt — as well as Jews, Christians, and Magians; they are the offspring of the Jann.

I say: The narrations regarding the story of this group of Jinn who listened to the Quran are numerous and vary greatly. There is no way to authenticate their texts through the Book or through reliable contextual clues; therefore, we have limited ourselves to the report of al-Qummi mentioned above. A portion of them will follow in the commentary of Surah al-Jinn, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

In the same source, the Learned One (عليه‌السلام) was asked about the believers among the Jinn: "Will they enter Paradise?" He replied: "No, but Allah has enclosures (حظائر) between Paradise and the Fire, in which the believers of the Jinn and the sinful among the Shi'ah will be."

I say: Something similar has been narrated in some mawquf narrations from the Sunni traditions. The narration of al-Qummi is mursal (مرسلة) — a narration where the link to the original source is omitted — and resembles a mudmarah (مضمرة) — a narration where the Imam is not explicitly named. If it is accepted, it should be interpreted as referring to the lowest levels of Paradise. The general statements of the Book indicate the universality of reward for the obedient among both humans and Jinn.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abi Ya'fur, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) saying: "The masters of the Prophets and Messengers are five, and they are the resolute among the messengers (أولوا العزم من الرسل), and upon them the millstone turns: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and upon all the Prophets."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The first legatee (وصي) on the face of the earth was Hibatullah [Seth], the son of Adam. There has not passed a prophet except that he had a legatee.

The total number of prophets was one hundred and twenty thousand prophets; among them were five who were resolute (أولوا العزم): Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم وعليهم)." [The tradition continues].

I say: That the resolute (أولوا العزم) are five is among the matters widely reported (مستفاضت) in the narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام). It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and from al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, and al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) through numerous paths.

From Rawdat al-Wa'izin by al-Mufid: It was said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "How much is between this world and the Hereafter?" He replied: "The blink of an eye. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ بَلاغٌ ‘The day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried but for an hour of a day. [This is] a delivery’ (46:35)."

(1) al-Majannah: The place of the Jinn.
